observations.recommendation:
To minimize reliance on
COS, JOs and similar
arrangement personnel
while creating income
opportunities for
constituents, we
recommended that
Management:
a. Invest in skills
development programs
that empower constituents
with the necessary
training and expertise to
qualify for permanent
positions. This can
include vocational
training, certification
programs, and workshops
tailored to local job
market demands.  
b. Facilitate
entrepreneurship support
programs that enable
constituents to start small
businesses or enterprises.
Provide access to
financial assistance,
business development
training, mentorship, and
networking opportunities
to help individuals establish
sustainable livelihoods.
c. Initiate community-
based projects that engage
constituents in income-
generating activities while
addressing local needs.
Collaborate with private
sector partners to create
employment opportunities.
d. Strengthen the capacity
of local organizations,
cooperatives, and
community groups to
engage in income-
generating activities
autonomously. Provide
technical assistance,
organizational support, and
access to markets to
empower constituents to
take ownership of their
economic development.
We also reiterate our
recommendation that
Management, through the
HRMO, conduct a
comprehensive review of
the current COS, JO and
similar arrangement
positions to evaluate their
necessity and relevance.
Ensure that the
recruitment adheres to the
limitations outlined by the
Joint Circular to mitigate
the increase in other
general services expenses.

observations.recommendation:
1.7. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
• Adjust the RPT Receivable and Deferred RPT Income in the
General Fund to conform with the records of the Municipal
Treasurer;
• Adjust the SET Receivables and the Deferred SET Income in the
Special Education Fund; and
• Determine the advance tax payments applicable in future years and
record the Deferred RPT/SET Income the RPT/SET to be earned in
future years.
1.8. We further recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to
furnish the Municipal Accountant a list showing of RPT and SET
collectible at the beginning of every year to facilitate the set-up of RPT
and SET collectible.

2.7. We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to reclassify
Semi-Expendable Properties within the PPE as follows:
• Those that are still in the custody of the Property Custodian,
reclassify to the appropriate Semi-Expendable Property account;
• Those acquired in CY 2024 but were already issued to the end-users,
reclassify to the appropriate Semi-Expendable Property Expense
and reverse the entries for the recognition of depreciation; and
• Those that were acquired prior to CY 2024 and were already issued
to the end-users, charge to Prior Period Adjustment the carrying
amount and close the corresponding accumulated depreciation.
2.8. We further recommended and the Property Custodian agreed to:
• Replace the existing Property Cards (PCs) maintained with Semi-
Expendable Property Cards (SPCs) for all items reclassified as
semi-expendable properties; and
• Replace the existing Acknowledgment Receipt for Equipment
(ARE) or Property Acknowledgment Receipt (PAR) with Inventory
Custodian Slips (ICS) for all reclassified semi-expendable
properties to accurately reflect accountability.
2.9. We finally recommended and Management agreed to require the
Inventory Committee to be mindful of Semi-Expendable Properties
during the physical count to properly identify and distinguish them
from PPE.

We recommended and the
Management agreed to
assess the Municipality's
expenditure controls and
procurement processes to
pinpoint opportunities for
optimizing or trimming
spending while
safeguarding essential
services. Implementing
cost-saving measures and
enhancing efficiency
across different municipal
departments and programs
can help ease financial
pressure and mitigate the
deficit.
Furthermore, we
recommended that the
Municipal Accountant
diligently oversee the
Municipality's financial
performance by
conducting thorough
analyses of financial
statements and budgetary
practices. This proactive
approach will enable the
identification of deficit-
contributing areas and
facilitate the
implementation of
corrective measures.

We restate our
recommendation from the
previous year that the
Municipal Accountant
only issue further cash
advances to the disbursing
officer if previous
advances have been
settled or proper
accounting procedures
have been followed in
accordance with the
established rules and
regulations.
We also recommended
that Management appoint
additional special
disbursing officers to
allow the existing DO to
focus solely on regular
cash advances and prevent
her from being
overwhelmed by
disbursing numerous
payrolls.

We recommended and the
Management agreed to
refrain from incurring
unauthorized expenses
from the 20 percent
Development Fund and
ensure strict adherence to
the prescribed guidelines
outlined in the JMC.

We recommended that the
Municipal Treasurer agreed
to consider allocating
resources and prioritize
setting aside cash to
transfer the unexpended
balance of the LDRRMF,
and the Municipal
Accountant promptly book
the transfer of the
unexpended balance of the
LDRRMF from the GF to the
TF at the end of the year,
consistent with applicable
rules and regulations.

We recommended that the
Management require the
LDRRMO and the
Municipal Accountant to
prepare a monthly Report
on the Utilization of the
LDRRMF and submit it to
the Audit Team within the
prescribed period.

We recommended and the
Management agreed to
review the budget
allocation for cultural
development and to ensure
that it adequately covers
festival-related expenses.
Consider adjusting the
budget as necessary to
prevent the need to rely on
the GAD fund for such
expenditures.
We also recommend that
the GAD Focal Person
ensure that programs,
projects, and activities not
related to addressing
gender issues be
separately funded by the
General Fund and see to it
that the use of the GAD
Fund complies with
relevant laws, regulations,
and guidelines governing
its allocation and
expenditure.

We recommended that the
Municipal Mayor or the
Procuring Entity:
a. Direct the end-users to
conduct project costing
through market research/study
of the goods to be procured,
consider other cost
components, and gather
information about the goods
required alongside the
identified needs and the
corresponding budget to
ensure that the ABC is set
within the reasonable and
fair cost of goods;
b. The BAC cease the practice
of patronizing favored
suppliers by faithfully and
widely disseminating the RFQs
to suppliers of known
qualifications or distributing
the invitations of bid to
prospective bidders to
intensify competition to avail
of the most competitive
prices of the goods; and
c. Require the IAS to review
the existing procurement
policies and procedures to
promote transparency,
competition, and fairness in
the procurement process.
Conduct price benchmarking
by comparing the prices paid
by the municipality with
prevailing market rates for
similar goods and call the
attention of the end-user and
the BAC if there are any
instances of overpricing.

We recommended and the
Management agreed to avoid
attaching the nicknames,
initials, or other
representations of the LCE
and other local officials to
items listed in the COA
Circular.
Additionally, we
recommended that
Management consider
removing any previously
affixed representations, if
feasible, to align with
regulatory guidelines.

We highly recommended,
therefore, that the
Management promptly
provides the audit team with
an adequate storage room
for vouchers and other
documents. This action is
essential not only to comply
with Section 20 of PD No.
1445 but also to ensure
efficient record-keeping and
facilitate proper audit
procedures.
We also recommended that
the Accounting and Property
Units ensure that all PPEs
included in the RPCPPE are
duly recorded in their
respective records and that
Property Cards (PC) and the
PPE Ledger Cards are
reconciled consistent with the
guidelines and procedures
stated in COA Circular No.
2020-006, dated January 31,
2020 to come up with PPE
balances that are verifiable
as to existence, condition and
accountability.

We recommended that the
Municipal Accountant
exclude revenue from the
own use of the heavy
equipment from the
Reported Income and
Expenses and recognize
non-cash and other valid
costs as part of the
expenses to assess
whether the rental operation
of the heavy equipment is
earning and properly
managed.
We also recommended
that the Municipal Mayor
thoroughly review the
rental operation of the
heavy equipment and, if

equipment to outside
clientele, instead utilize it

We recommended that the
HRM Officer and the
Municipal Accountant ensure
that valid and justifiable
documents supporting the

leave credits claim for

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Inventory Committee to undertake and complete the physical inventory of the municipality’s PPEs and all the procedures for the one-time cleansing of the PPE account balances and submit the Report of the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE); and We also recommended that the Accounting and Property Units ensure that all PPEs included in the RPCPPE are duly recorded in their respective records and that Property Cards (PC) and the PPE Ledger Cards are reconciled consistent with the guidelines and procedures stated in COA Circular No. 2020-006, dated January 31, 2020 to come up with PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.
observations.reason: Although the physical inventory was conducted in CY 2023, reconciliation between the RPCPPE and the PPE Ledger Cards was not performed.

observations.recommendation:
1.10 We recommended that Management:
a. Designate a full-time property officer to ensure consistent oversight and accurate Management of PPE. This appointment will facilitate timely updates to PPE records, reducing discrepancies between physical inventories and financial statements.
b. The Municipal Treasurer actively engages in property management to bolster oversight and accountability regarding PPE assets, ultimately aiming to reconcile both records.
c. Establish a system where property stickers are affixed to each PPE item to facilitate the reconciliation process by providing a transparent and efficient method for tracking and identifying assets during inventory counts.; and
d. Undertake the required reconciliation of property records following the guidelines and procedures in COA Circular No. 2020-006 and effect the necessary adjustments to present the PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition, and accountability.

2.7 We recommended that Management identify opportunities to reduce operating expenses and improve cost efficiency. This may include streamlining administrative processes or eliminating non-essential expenses to free up cash flow and ensure compliance with legal disbursement limits.
Likewise, Management implements effective cash management practices to optimize cash flow and liquidity. This may include establishing cash reserves, improving accounts receivable and payable Management, and implementing cash flow monitoring tools to track cash inflows and outflows in real-time

3.6 We recommended and the Management agreed to assess the Municipality's expenditure controls and procurement processes to pinpoint opportunities for optimizing or trimming spending while safeguarding essential services. Implementing cost-saving measures and enhancing efficiency across different municipal departments and programs can help ease financial pressure and mitigate the deficit.

Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant diligently oversee the Municipality's financial performance by conducting thorough analyses of financial statements and budgetary practices. This proactive approach will enable the identification of deficit-contributing areas and facilitate the implementation of corrective measures.

We recommended that the Accounting Unit recognize the outstanding receivables amounting to ₱1.135 million in the books of account. We also recommend that henceforth, ensure that receivables are recorded in the books and supported with complete subsidiary schedules consistent with the accounting standards.

We recommended that Management, through the Accounting Unit and the Human Resources and Management Office, review and streamline payroll processes to enhance efficiency, accuracy, and timeliness in management. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the cash advances are based on the net amount owed by employees for a given pay period instead of estimated amounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to refrain from recording the liquidation of the payroll if not all payees have signed the payrolls and ensure that the payrolls submitted by the DO is thus complete. We also recommended that the DO or the Accounting Unit submit the missing payrolls to the Audit Team for post-audit.

We recommended and the Management agreed to ensure that special cash advances are supported with a breakdown of cost estimates and supporting documents. We also recommend that Management carefully review and verify the estimates to ensure they are reasonable and in line with the purpose of the cash advance in accordance with the rules and regulations on granting cash advances.

We recommended and the Acting Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure that a special cash advance should only be granted to duly designated disbursing officer. Elected officials’ cash advances should only be limited to their official travel expenses consistent with the COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997.

We recommended and the Management agreed to instruct the offices involved in the distribution of goods to submit the original copies of the lists of goods to the beneficiaries or recipients as a basis for confirmation. Also, the Accounting Office must ensure that the itemized list of goods distributed is attached to the DVs for easy verification.

We recommended that the Management, through the Municipal Accountant, request from the DILG authority for study tours or related activities. We also recommended that Management strictly observe the requirements conformably with the existing study tours or benchmarking guidelines.

We recommended and the Management agreed to assess their specific requirements, budgetary constraints, and the intended usage of the laptops before making procurement decisions to avoid unnecessary and highly excessive purchases.

We recommended and the Management agreed to ensure that the PO or contract with fuel suppliers is based on a retail pump price instead of the retail fixed price. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ascertain that payment of POL is at the retail pump price consistent with the existing regulations.

We recommended, and the Management agreed to ensure that the VM, SB Members, and their staff’s official travel are authorized by the Municipal Mayor, consistent with the rules and regulations on official trips. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that the Municipal Mayor duly authorizes the legislative officials and their staff's official travel before processing their traveling expense claims.

We recommended and the Management agreed to consider the immediate disposal of unserviceable and obsolete properties. After the disposal, drop from the books of accounts the disposed property and take up any proceeds from the sale thereof, in compliance with paragraph 67 of PAS 16 and section 79 of PD 1445.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this year’s report.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management direct all concerned officials especially the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Engineer to support all issuances of construction materials with necessary documents.

The Municipal Accountant shall see to it that each completed project implemented by the administration must be supported with a duly signed Accomplishment Report, Pre and Post Inspection Report, and photos (before, during, and after) to document the actual repair and maintenance undertaken for each project.
observations.reason: In the post-audit of CY-2021 disbursement vouchers, we noticed that the required supporting documents were still not attached to the disbursement vouchers. During the exit conference, a representative from the Mayor’s Office has assured to call once again the attention of the Municipal Engineer on this issue.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the Acting Municipal Treasurer agreed to assign a regular and duly bonded employee to handle land tax-collecting functions in accordance with the existing revenue regulation.
observations.reason: The prevailing condition still exists based on the recent cash examination conducted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Management sensibly review the rental operation of the heavy equipment and present remedial courses of actions to generate income instead of losses from the rental operation.  Otherwise, stop renting out the heavy equipment and instead utilize it for Municipality’s projects.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this year’s report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management require the Accounting Office to enforce the collection of outstanding receivables by issuing demand letters to all debtors with past due accounts.

We also recommended that the LGU through the Sanggunian Bayan revisit the related Ordinance regulating the use and rental of heavy equipment and provide adequate provisions on the collection of receivables especially those past due accounts, and the imposition of penalties or sanctions against the defaulting debtors.

Furthermore, we recommended that the LGU establish a strong internal control as regards the collection of receivables to sustain the viability of revenue operation.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this year’s report

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management exert diligent efforts to collect the long outstanding receivables to sustain the continuity of the livelihood program, and locate the supporting documents necessary to establish the existence and validity of the receivable accounts so that possible remedies may be considered to enforce collection.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary ledgers containing the complete information of loans, to monitor collections of amortizations from concerned NGOs/POs and individuals.
observations.reason:
Receivables still exist in the books.

During the exit conference, the Management has assured to review the accounts and, if warranted, will process the request for authority to write off the dormant accounts.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to recognize AP in the books only when the goods are accepted or services are rendered, and the corresponding bills are received from the contractor or supplier.
We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer meantime earmarks the amount of Purchase Order in the Registry to confirm that it is covered with appropriation but records only the obligation when the agency incurs actual commitment.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant, and the Inventory Committee agreed to undertake the necessary reconciliation of property records in accordance with the guidelines and procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2020-006 and effect the necessary adjustments to fairly present the affected accounts in the financial statement.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor requires the Inventory Committee to undertake the required physical count of inventory items, effect the necessary adjustments, and submit to the Auditor an updated inventory report not later than January 31 of each year in accordance with the existing regulation.

We recommended and the Supply and Property Officer agreed to consider the immediate disposal of unserviceable and obsolete properties. After the disposal, drop from the books of accounts the values of the disposed properties and take up the proceeds from sales thereof, in compliance with the existing regulations.

We recommended and the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer agreed to furnish the Municipal Accountant with a detailed list of donations in-kind received by the Municipality for proper recognition in the books in accordance with existing regulations.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor sensibly review the rental operation of the heavy equipment and present remedial courses of action to generate income instead of losses from the rental operation. Otherwise, stop renting out the heavy equipment and instead utilize it solely for Municipality's projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer or the Municipal Accountant to enforce the collection of outstanding receivables by issuing demand letters to all debtors with past due accounts; otherwise, resort to legal actions against defaulting debtors.
We also recommended that the Sanggunian Bayan revisit the Ordinance regulating the use and rental of heavy equipment and provide adequate provisions on the collection of receivables and the imposition of penalties or sanctions against the defaulting debtors.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Offices concerned to strictly comply with the preparation and accomplishment of Driver’s Trip Tickets and the Item Availment Form indicating therein the information required.
We also recommended that the Municipality formulate guidelines to monitor, check and control the issuance of fuel, oil, and lubricants as well as gas consumption of various offices with the accredited gas stations so that rules and regulations as provided by law shall be strictly followed.

We recommended that Management through the Municipal Planning and Development Officer, strictly complies with the provisions regarding the allocation and utilization of the 20% Development Fund.
We also recommended and the Municipal Budget Officer agreed to appropriately charge meals and accommodation expenses to General Fund and not to the 20% Development Fund, otherwise, require the concerned officials to refund the amount disbursed.

We recommended that the person in charge of KALAHI projects execute a Memorandum of Agreement between the LGU and the recipient barangays and submit a copy to the Audit Team, including other necessary documents for reference in the audit.

Lastly, require the Municipal Treasurer to secure Official Receipts from recipient barangays in acknowledgment of the fund transferred to them.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, through the Local Sanggunian issues internal guidelines to rationalize and regulate the provision of communication allowance to officials and employees.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant to:
Revert to the general fund, the amount of P45,636,583.64 obligated as payable without proper documentation, and which had been outstanding for more than two years already in accordance to COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 99-6.
Henceforth, strictly comply with the regulation on the proper booking up of obligations according to Section 4(s) of the NGAS Manual, Volume I, to fairly present the Accounts Payable in the financial statements at year-end.

We recommended that Management require the Accounting Office to enforce the collection of outstanding receivables by issuing demand letters to all debtors with past due accounts.
We also recommended that the LGU through the Sanggunian Bayan revisit the related Ordinance regulating the use and rental of heavy equipment and provide adequate provisions on the collection of receivables especially those past due accounts, and the imposition of penalties or sanctions against the defaulting debtors.
Furthermore, we recommended that the LGU establish a strong internal control as regards the collection of receivables to sustain the viability of revenue operation.

We recommended that Management exert diligent efforts to collect the long outstanding receivables to sustain the continuity of the livelihood program, and locate the supporting documents necessary to establish the existence and validity of the receivable accounts, so that possible remedies maybe considered to enforce collection.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary ledgers containing the complete information of loans, to monitor collections of amortizations from concerned NGOs/POs and individuals.

We recommended that Management require the Accounting Office and Property Officer to undertake the required reconciliation of property records under Section 114 of Manual on NGAS, Volume I and effect the necessary adjustments to fairly present the said accounts in the financial statement.
We also recommended that the Property Officer determine ownership of all unrecorded property and equipment found during the physical count, prepare/issue Property Acknowledgement Receipt to the end-user of the item, and record the same in the PEC and RPCPPE.  The RPCPPE should include a separate caption of the unrecorded property and equipment found in the station and this shall be the basis of the Accounting Office in recording the subject property and equipment in the books of accounts.
Furthermore, the Accounting Office and Property Officer investigate the noted differences and if found that these properties are missing, hold the accountable officer liable thereof.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to draw a journal entry voucher to correct the previous entries made on unexpended/unobligated capital outlay (CO) balance of the LDRRMF and henceforth, observe the proper accounting and reporting guidelines on the said fund.
We recommended that Management require the LDRRM Office to prepare and submit a duly certified Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF using the prescribed format. Likewise, the LDRRM and Accounting Office to reconcile their figures for a fair presentation of reported balances in the FS.

We recommended that Management exert effort on titling ten (10) parcels of land in the name of the municipal government.  Likewise, for six other parcels of land which were not yet fully paid by the Municipality, require the seller to transfer the title in the name of the LGU before full payment is made.

We recommended that Management submits the lacking documents above-mentioned, otherwise be held personally liable for the amount disbursed as it is contrary to existing guidelines on the grant of CNA incentives. Henceforth, observe strictly the provisions of DBM Budget Circular No. 2009-5.
We recommended that Management require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the BRS and submit the same to the Auditor for verification and accurate reporting of the Cash in Bank balance in the FS.

We recommended that Management submits the original copy of the distribution list of goods to the beneficiaries or recipients as a basis for confirmation.  Henceforth, attached the itemized list of good distributed for ease of verification.

We recommended that Management require the HRMO Officer to strictly comply with Sections 22 and 23, Rule XVI of the Omnibus Rules Implementing Book V of Executive Order No. 292 and Civil Service Commission Resolution No. 00034 and ensure that the Application for Leave Form is fully accomplished as regards the monetization of leave credits.

We recommended that Management ensure that the proper procedures in the release, utilization, reporting, and audit of confidential expenses are observed and in accordance with Joint Circular No. 2015-01.

We recommended that Management require the Accountable Officers to refund the amount of P58,048.68 for the penalty incurred by the Municipality.  Likewise, loan payment shall be given priority and shall be paid in full on or before the due date to avoid the unnecessary penalty charges.

Therefore, we recommended that the Management direct the Offices concerned to expedite the implementation of all projects and ensure that projects to be funded out of the 20% DF were systematically planned and prioritized according to the most urgent need by the locality to avoid further reprogramming or realignment.

In view, therefore, we recommended that the Municipality ensure that monies held in trust be used only for their intended purposes. The Municipality, specifically the Local School Board to exercise prudence in the utilization of the available resources and ensure that only highly necessary expenditures are paid.

Given the foregoing, we recommended that Management ensure that the GAD Plan and Accomplishment Report contains a quantifiable performance result comparable with the performance indicator or target to gauge the agency's accomplishment during the year.
In view, therefore, we recommended that the Municipality prepares and submits the LSWMP and ensure that the identified projects, programs, and activities to be implemented are based on the latter.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer immediately restitute/deposit the missing funds totaling P1,867,072.57;

Likewise, the Municipal Mayor shall:

Cause the withholding of the Municipal Treasurer’s salaries and other monies due to her; Relieve her of her duties as liquidating officer; and c) File appropriate charges against her for failure to perform her duties honestly and diligently and to present the missing funds upon demand.

Furthermore, the Municipal Mayor designate a responsible liquidating officer and see to it that he/she shall be closely supervised by the Municipal Mayor to ensure that the new liquidating officer deposits all his/her collections intact daily or not later than the next banking day as required under Section 69 of PD 1445, and related COA issuance, and to avoid the commission of similar shortages and/or occurrence of risks of loss of public funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:

Direct all concerned officials/employees to coordinate with the end-users of the project during the planning phase to fully define their needs;

Advise the Bids and Awards Committee and concerned personnel in-charge of procurement to revisit the procurement process, identify points of possible delay and establish measures to address the flaw to avoid recurrence of delay in the future similar projects; and

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Treasurer stop the practice of pre-signed checks.
The Municipal Mayor designate proper officials to act as counter-signatories of checks in case where the Municipal Treasurer or the Municipal Mayor is temporarily absent or on official travel so as not to hamper the operation of the LGU.
We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer to maintain the Cashbook-Cash in Bank for all funds and to regularly update such cashbook for proper monitoring of Cash in Bank balance.

We recommended that each Collecting Officer of the LGU prepare and submit to the Audit Team the Monthly Report of Accountability for accountable forms.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare and submit the consolidated Monthly Report of Accountability for accountable forms in conformity with the above-mentioned regulation.

We recommended that the LGU exert diligent efforts to collect the long outstanding receivables to sustain the continuity of the livelihood program.  At the same time, the LGU to locate the supporting documents necessary to establish the existence and validity of the receivable accounts and consider all possible remedies in the collection of these receivables.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary ledgers containing the complete information of loans to monitor collections of amortizations from concerned NGOs/POs and individuals.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, thru the Municipal Planning and Development Office to ensure that the Municipality’s Annual Investment Plan would contain the detailed breakdown of each PPAs to be implemented and facilitate its inclusion in the Annual Budget.

We recommended that the LGU consider all possible remedies to enforce liquidation of financial assistance granted.  Moreover, for future fund transfer or financial assistance to NGOs/POs, adhere strictly to the provisions set forth under COA Circular 2007-001 to ensure the availability of complete data and information when needed by authorities.  Likewise, the accounting office should maintain subsidiary ledgers containing the complete information related to any financial assistance for accounting, monitoring and control purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant records the funds transferred to barangays to “Due from LGU’s” account and require full liquidation of this fund to adhere with the provisions of COA Circular 2004-008.

We recommended that the Inventory Committee complete the inventory taking and render a report thereon on the agency's PPE.  Subsequently, their Inventory Report should be reconciled with the existing records kept by the Accounting Office.  Any discrepancies between the report and records should be further investigated until both are reconciled.
We also recommended that the Inventory Committee utilize the subsidiary ledgers maintained by the Accounting Office in the inventory taking and later reconcile their physical inventory report against that of the subsidiary records.
The Accounting Office should religiously maintain the PPELC to provide a detailed information of the PPE which can be of great importance in the conduct of the physical inventory.
Likewise, the Property Custodian to provide and attach property sticker to each PPE item for ease of reference and property tagging.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall allocate 1% of the Municipality’s IRA as provided for under Section 15 of RA 9344 to ensure that the LGU’s LCPC can adopt a comprehensive plan on delinquency prevention and overseeing its proper implementation.

We recommended that:
The Municipal Accountant reverts back all continuing appropriation to the General Fund and advice the MDRRM Council through the MDRRM Officer to implement the previously identified capital outlay projects;
The Municipal Accounting Office provides a breakdown of the 70% Mitigation Fund (MOOE and CO) in the Report on Utilization of DRRMF for proper monitoring and utilization of the fund.
The Municipal Disaster and Risk Reduction Management Council consider in the formulation of disaster risk reduction management programs/projects/activities the previous year's unexpended balance of LDRRMF to attain maximum utilization of the fund; and,
The Municipal Mayor require the Municipal LDRRMO and/or the Municipal Accountant to regularly prepare a monthly Report on the Utilization of the LDRRMF and submit the same to the Audit Team within the period prescribed by the above regulation for immediate audit action so that corresponding result may be brought to the attention of concerned officials the soonest possible time for their immediate and appropriate action.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause of the unreconciled amounts.  Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balances of the affected accounts.  Henceforth, exercise prudence in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of reciprocal accounts are at all times equal.

We recommended that the BAC to undertake and prepare the Post-Qualification Evaluation Report while the TWG should only assist in the process.  We further recommend that the BAC create several TWG with at least three members to handle different types of procurement.  These all to effectively study the requirements and evaluate the bids submitted by bidders in consonance with the mandate of the 2016 R-IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
Direct all concerned officials/employees to coordinate with the end-users of the project during the planning phase in order to fully define their needs;
Advise the Bids and Awards Committee and concerned personnel in-charge of procurement to revisit the procurement process, identify points of possible delay and establish measures to address the flaw to avoid recurrence of delay in the future similar projects; and

Likewise, install a biometric machine in every office building for ease of access. These all, to establish effective control over the attendance of each employee.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendations:

We recommended that the Inventory Committee to complete the inventory taking
and render a report thereon on the agency’s PPE. Subsequently, their Inventory
Report should be reconciled with the existing records kept by the Accounting
Office. Any discrepancies between the report and records should be further
investigated until both are reconciled. These all to confirm the existence of the
PPEs having a stated carrying value in the Financial Statements.

We also recommended that the Inventory Committee utilize the subsidiary
ledgers maintained by the Accounting Office in the inventory taking and later
reconcile their physical inventory report against that of the subsidiary records.

The Accounting Office should religiously maintain the PPELC to provide a
detailed information of the PPE which can be of great importance in the conduct
of the physical inventory.

Likewise, the Property Custodian to provide and attach property sticker to each
PPE item for ease of reference and property tagging.

Recommendation:

We recommend and the Property Custodian-Designate agreed to immediately
initiate the disposal of all unserviceable vehicles in accordance with the modes
prescribed under COA Circular No. 89-296 to avoid further deterioration and to

decongest the parking garage.

Recommendations:

We recommended that all local revenue collectors should remit intact and
promptly all their collections to the liquidating officer/municipal treasurer and
the latter should deposit the collections turned over to her daily as provided in
Sec.15 and 32, respectively, of the Manual on NGAS. The Municipal Treasurer
being the custodian of government fund should faithfully adhere to this
observation.

Likewise, the agency may withhold payment of salary of accountable officers who
are not depositing collection in full and intact and impose administrative sanction,
if warranted, to ultimately end said practice.

Recommendation:

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer to effect the cancellation of staled
checks in accordance Section 56 of the NGAS Manual. Once cancelled, the
Municipal Accountant to immediately draw the Journal Entry Vouchers to
reclassify all cancelled checks from Cash in Bank account to Accounts Payable
account to adjust the respective account balances in the Financial Statements.

Recommendations:

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Offices concerned to
expedite the implementation of all projects funded by 20% DF to ensure optimum
utilization of the fund.

The Municipal Mayor to assign an Office or personnel to conduct regular
monitoring of project implementation so that the Municipality can determine the
efficiency of project implementation.

Ensure that projects to be funded out of the 20% DF were systematically planned
and prioritized according to the most urgent need by the locality to avoid further
realignment

Management Comment:
The Municipal Mayor has proposed to increase the Petty Cash Fund to allow the
reimbursement or purchase of minor supplies and unanticipated expenses. He also
agreed to grant a special cash advance for financial assistance.

Recommendations:

a. Reimbursement must be avoided as this violates the standards set forth in the

2016 Revised-IRR of RA No. 9184;

b. Henceforth, payment of operating expenses and procurement of goods and

services should be directly to suppliers or service providers through issuance of
checks. Petty expenses can be charged to Petty Cash Fund and;

¢. Payments of financial assistance should be made directly to recipient through

check or if not practical, can be paid out of the special cash advance.

brief all officials of the city, and prepare a checklist of the required documents for
dissemination. For deficiency noted while processing, a deficiency slip shall be
attached to the voucher and returned to the appropriate department for
compliance.

the utilization of the GAD Fund to fully address the gender issues at hand.
